perturbation.original_text: and (e) Confidential Information is hereby acknowledged by CONSULTANT to be the sole property of NYNEX and shall be returned to NYNEX (including, without limitation, all materials, documents, drawings, models, apparatus, sketches, designs, specifications and lists, encompassing or evidencing same or related thereto, and all copies/formats thereof), within seven (7) days after receipt by CONSULTANT of a written request from NYNEX setting forth the Confidential Information to be returned.
perturbation.changed_text: and (e) Confidential Information is hereby acknowledged by CONSULTANT to be the sole property of NYNEX and shall be returned to NYNEX (including, without limitation, all materials, documents, drawings, models, apparatus, sketches, designs, specifications and lists, encompassing or evidencing same or related thereto, and all copies/formats thereof), within two (2) days after receipt by CONSULTANT of a written request from NYNEX setting forth the Confidential Information to be returned.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return period of confidential information from 7 days to 2 days after request may be unreasonable and contradict legal standards for providing sufficient time to comply with contractual obligations. Some jurisdictions may view 2 days as insufficient for a reasonable effort to gather and return all materials.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CVP) § 2214
perturbation.law_explanation: CVP § 2214 generally requires that a notice of motion be served at least eight days before the time specified for the hearing. While this pertains to court motions, it sets a precedent that statutory notices require at least 8 days, and this contract provision reduces the time to 2 days. Depending on the volume of confidential information, two days might be considered an unreasonably short timeframe to fulfill the request, and therefore renders the clause unenforceable under general principles of reasonableness and fairness, which are implicit in contract interpretation under New York law.

Motion papers; service; time. (a) Notice of motion. A notice of motion shall specify the time and place of the hearing on the motion, the supporting papers upon which the motion is based, the relief demanded and the grounds therefor. Relief in the alternative or of several different types may be demanded. (b) Time for service of notice and affidavits. A notice of motion and supporting affidavits shall be served at least eight days before the time at which the motion is noticed to be heard. Answering affidavits shall be served at least two days before such time. Answering affidavits and any notice of cross-motion, with supporting papers, if any, shall be served at least seven days before such time if a notice of motion served at least sixteen days before such time so demands; whereupon any reply or responding affidavits shall be served at least one day before such time. (c) Furnishing papers to the court. Each party shall furnish to the court all papers served by that party. The moving party shall furnish all other papers not already in the possession of the court necessary to the consideration of the questions involved. Except when the rules of the court provide otherwise, in an e-filed action, a party that files papers in connection with a motion need not include copies of papers that were filed previously electronically with the court, but may make reference to them, giving the docket numbers on the e-filing system. Where such papers are in the possession of an adverse party, they shall be produced by that party at the hearing on notice served with the motion papers. Only papers served in accordance with the provisions of this rule shall be read in support of, or in opposition to, the motion, unless the court for good cause shall otherwise direct. (d) Order to show cause. The court in a proper case may grant an order to show cause, to be served in lieu of a notice of mo
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the timeframe for returning confidential information from seven days to two days, which introduces a contradiction with the principle of reasonableness and fairness in contract interpretation. The Law Explanation argues that two days might be an unreasonably short timeframe, potentially rendering the clause unenforceable. The provided snippet from CVP § 2214, although related to motion papers, illustrates that legal notices often require a longer timeframe (at least eight days). This supports the argument that a two-day period for returning confidential information may be insufficient and therefore contradicts the implicit requirement of reasonableness within contract law.

perturbation.original_text: CONSULTANT warrants and represents that CONSULTANT has bound its employees, agents and subcontractors to the terms and conditions of this Agreement or that each and every employee, agent or subcontractor has personally executed a Non-Disclosure Agreement containing terms and conditions no less stringent than the terms and conditions contained herein; and furthermore CONSULTANT hereby <PAGE> agrees to indemnify, hold harmless and defend NYNEX from and against any loss, cost, damage, expense or claim arising out of any breach of this provision or the failure of CONSULTANT or its employees, agents or subcontractors to protect such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: CONSULTANT warrants and represents that CONSULTANT has bound its employees, agents and subcontractors to the terms and conditions of this Agreement or that each and every employee, agent or subcontractor has personally executed a Non-Disclosure Agreement containing terms and conditions no less stringent than the terms and conditions contained herein; and furthermore CONSULTANT hereby <PAGE> agrees to indemnify, hold harmless and defend NYNEX from and against any loss, cost, damage, expense or claim arising out of any gross negligence on their part to protect such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the indemnity to cases of 'gross negligence', the clause now contradicts the broader legal standards for indemnification, which often extend to ordinary negligence depending on the specific jurisdiction and the nature of the agreement. This undermines the protection NYNEX would have under a standard indemnification clause, where liability can be found on ordinary negligence. Making it only gross negligence is a high bar to clear in litigation.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 5-322.1 generally prohibits agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence in connection with construction, maintenance, and repair. While this NDA is not directly related to those activities, the underlying principle is that parties cannot contract away liability for their own negligence. Limiting indemnity to 'gross negligence' could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent this principle by setting a higher bar for liability, making it more difficult for NYNEX to recover damages resulting from the consultant's negligence (short of gross negligence) in protecting confidential information. Therefore, it potentially contradicts the spirit and public policy reflected in NY GOL § 5-322.1.

Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases. 1. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to indemnify or hold harmless the promisee against liability for damage arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property contributed to, caused by or resulting from the negligence of the promisee, his agents or employees, or indemnitee, whether such negligence be in whole or in part, is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this section shall not affect the validity of any insurance contract, workers' compensation agreement or other agreement issued by an admitted insurer. This subdivision shall not preclude a promisee requiring indemnification for damages arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property caused by or resulting from the negligence of a party other than the promisee, whether or not the promisor is partially negligent. 2. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to condition a subcontractor's or materialman's right to file a claim and/or commence an action on a payment bond on exhaustion of another legal remedy is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this subdivision shall not affect the validity of any insu
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the indemnification obligation from any breach to only 'gross negligence'. The Law Explanation suggests this may contradict the principle in NY GOL § 5-322.1, which prohibits agreements exempting parties from liability for negligence. The law snippet provided, NY GOL § 5-322.1, states agreements indemnifying parties against liability for damage caused by their own negligence are void. By limiting the indemnity to gross negligence, the modified contract attempts to circumvent this principle by raising the bar for liability, contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: This Non-Disclosure Agreement shall become effective upon the day and year first written, and shall remain in effect until terminated in writing by either party. Notwithstanding any such termination, the rights and obligations with respect to the disclosure of Confidential Information set forth herein shall survive the termination of this Non-Disclosure Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Non-Disclosure Agreement shall become effective upon the day and year first written, and shall be immediately terminated upon written notice by either party. Notwithstanding any such termination, the rights and obligations with respect to the disclosure of Confidential Information set forth herein shall be terminated.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the clause to state that obligations concerning confidential information are terminated immediately upon the termination of the agreement might be considered unenforceable. Most NDAs have a survival clause to protect information disclosed during the agreement term, even after termination, to protect information that has already been shared. Immediately terminating the Confidentiality agreement leaves no protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Uniform Trade Secrets Act (N.Y. Gen. Bus. Law § 350)
perturbation.law_explanation: The New York Uniform Trade Secrets Act (N.Y. Gen. Bus. Law § 350) provides legal protection for trade secrets, which are often the subject of confidentiality agreements. The change suggests that all obligations with respect to Confidential Information are terminated, meaning trade secrets and other confidential info would not be protectable, immediately. This contradicts the purpose of the law.
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction. The original text states that obligations regarding confidential information survive the termination of the NDA, while the changed text states that these obligations are terminated immediately upon termination of the agreement. The Law Explanation states that the NY Uniform Trade Secrets Act provides legal protection for trade secrets, which are often the subject of confidentiality agreements. By terminating all obligations immediately, the changed text eliminates any protection for trade secrets, contradicting the purpose of the law. While the provided law snippet does not mention trade secrets or confidentiality, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation make it clear that immediately terminating the Confidentiality agreement leaves no protection, creating a direct legal contradiction.
